skill.name: zentao-tour
skill.description: Guides users through ZenTao onboarding with role-based tours that use zentao-cli to explore products, projects, tasks, bugs, test cases, and status flows.
skill.summary: Role-based ZenTao and zentao-cli onboarding for project teams.
skill.author: easysoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, weak-crypto, and entropy issues, but the command and entropy hits are mostly Markdown examples, Mermaid syntax, Chinese prose, and documentation links. The confirmed risk is legitimate but elevated: the skill asks an agent to run zentao-cli against a live ZenTao instance, including create, update, state transition, and delete operations after user confirmation.

medium_findings.title: Live ZenTao Data Mutation Through CLI Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to create products, stories, plans, projects, executions, tasks, test cases, bugs, and to perform status transitions in a real ZenTao environment. The workflow requires user consent before write operations, so this is legitimate product behavior, but a mistaken confirmation could alter production project data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files contain explicit zentao-cli create, update, resolve, close, start, and finish examples. The risk is moderated because SKILL.md also requires consent before create, update, delete, and status-flow commands.

medium_findings.title: Credential And Token Handling In Setup Guidance
medium_findings.description: The onboarding guide shows ZenTao login commands, environment variables, token-based MCP configuration, and a local credential cache path. This is normal setup guidance, but users could expose credentials if they paste real secrets into shared prompts, logs, or configuration files with weak local permissions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly references passwords, tokens, environment variables, and local credential cache paths. I found no exfiltration behavior, so this remains a handling and disclosure risk rather than malicious activity.

low_findings.title: Static Command-Execution Findings Are Mostly Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The scanner classified Markdown backticks and command snippets as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These are documentation examples for zentao-cli and Mermaid diagrams, not embedded executable code in the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows Markdown code fences and inline command text, not Ruby source or dynamic shell execution primitives. The commands are intended examples for an AI assistant to run only within the tour workflow.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation And Example Endpoints
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to the zentao-cli repository, ZenTao documentation, and a placeholder ZenTao server. I found no evidence that the skill sends hidden data to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed URLs are visible documentation links or example configuration values. No hidden network call, webhook, or credential exfiltration instruction was found.

low_findings.title: Filesystem And Weak-Crypto Alerts Appear To Be False Positives
low_findings.description: Filesystem alerts are caused by relative Markdown links and documented local configuration paths. Weak-cryptography and entropy alerts appear to be triggered by Markdown filenames, MCP configuration text, Chinese content, and dense command tables rather than cryptographic code or encoded payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation references or benign config paths, and there is no code implementing cryptography or decoding payloads. The entropy score is consistent with non-English prose and compact CLI syntax.

dangerous_patterns.title: State-Changing CLI Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes CLI patterns for create, update, delete, start, finish, resolve, and close operations. These operations are expected for the tour, but marketplace users should understand that they can modify live ZenTao records.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command patterns are explicit and repeated in role quick-reference tables. Consent instructions reduce abuse likelihood but do not remove the operational risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Token And Password Configuration Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup guide references password login, environment variables, token configuration, and a cached credentials file. These examples are not malicious, but they require careful secret handling by users and assistant sessions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines clearly mention credential-bearing inputs and local config paths. I found no instruction to transmit those secrets outside the configured ZenTao service.

content.user_title: Explore ZenTao with Guided Role Tours
content.value_statement: New ZenTao users often struggle to connect concepts, commands, and real workflows. This skill guides them through role-based tours using zentao-cli and simple conversation.
actual_capabilities: Checks zentao-cli readiness with profile and product list commands.
Routes users into product manager, project manager, tester, developer, or executive tours.
Provides ZenTao command examples for products, stories, plans, projects, executions, tasks, bugs, and test cases.
Explains common task and bug status transitions in user-friendly language.
Prompts for confirmation before create, update, delete, and status-change operations.
Introduces CLI installation, login, environment variables, and MCP configuration options.
limitations: Requires a reachable ZenTao instance and a working zentao-cli setup.
Can modify live ZenTao data when users approve write commands.
CLI examples may need adjustment for local ZenTao versions and permissions.
Does not replace official ZenTao administration, backup, or access-control guidance.

use_cases.title: Onboard a New Product Manager
use_cases.description: Turn a product idea into a ZenTao product, several stories, and a first product plan.

use_cases.title: Practice Delivery Flow
use_cases.description: Create a project, add a sprint, split stories into tasks, and review task progress.

use_cases.title: Learn Quality Workflow
use_cases.description: Walk through test cases, bugs, task ownership, resolution, and closure flows.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Tour
prompt_templates.prompt: Take me on a ZenTao tour. First check whether zentao-cli is ready, then help me choose the right role path.

prompt_templates.title: Try Product Planning
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me as a product manager. Help me create a sample product, write a few stories, and place them into a plan.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Sprint Walkthrough
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through a project manager workflow in ZenTao. Use an existing product, create a project and sprint, then split work into tasks.

prompt_templates.title: Review Executive Metrics
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the executive view to inspect project pace and product health. Keep it read-only and summarize the most important signals.

output_examples.input: I am new to ZenTao and want to see what it can do.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks your active ZenTao account, asks which team role fits you, and starts a short guided path for that role.

output_examples.input: Show me how a tester works in ZenTao.
output_examples.output: The assistant helps select a story, draft test cases, create a test task, and demonstrate the bug lifecycle with confirmation before changes.

output_examples.input: I only want a management overview.
output_examples.output: The assistant uses read-only queries to summarize active projects, product health, releases, feedback, or tickets based on your chosen focus.

best_practices: Use a sandbox or demo ZenTao workspace for first-time tours.
Confirm every write or delete command before execution.
Keep tokens and passwords out of shared prompts, screenshots, and logs.
anti_patterns: Do not run the tour against production data without backups or permission.
Do not paste real ZenTao passwords into public or shared assistant sessions.
Do not skip role selection and force every user through the same workflow.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps users learn ZenTao through guided role-based tours that use zentao-cli commands.

faq.question: Does it require zentao-cli?
faq.answer: Yes. The tour is built around zentao-cli checks, commands, and optional MCP configuration.

faq.question: Can it change my ZenTao data?
faq.answer: Yes. Some role tours create or update records after confirmation. Use a demo workspace when learning.

faq.question: Which roles are supported?
faq.answer: It supports product manager, project manager, tester, developer, executive, and a flexible exploratory path.

faq.question: Is the executive path read-only?
faq.answer: Yes. The executive role file explicitly says the path is read-only and should not trigger create, update, or delete actions.

faq.question: How should credentials be handled?
faq.answer: Use secure local configuration when possible. Avoid sharing passwords, tokens, or cached credential files in prompts or logs.
